source_title: Arthur Brooks On The Crisis Of Meaning & How To Actually Find It

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: Arthur Brooks is a Harvard professor and social scientist, one of the sharpest minds on what actually makes people happy. He teaches us about the three macronutrients of happiness: enjoyment, satisfaction, and meaning. This third macronutrient, meaning, is something the modern world is fairly inhospitable to. It acts as an antagonist to meaning. When did you first cotton on to the fact that there was a crisis of meaning afoot?

paragraphs.speaker: arthur
paragraphs.name: Arthur Brooks
paragraphs.text: I came back to academia in 2019. I'd been gone for a while, ran a think tank in Washington, D.C., for 11 years. When I came back, it was a different world. In 2008, college students were happier than people not on campus. They were falling in love, making friends, experimenting with ideas, partying. But in 2019, depression rates had tripled. Clinical anxiety was up by a factor of two. There was loneliness, protest, identity politics, cancel culture. As a behavioral scientist, I wanted to know what caused this. When you see a lot of unhappiness, you got to figure out what's actually happening. When there's not happiness, when you see a lot of misery, there's a blockage of one of these three macronutrients. So we've got an enjoyment problem, a satisfaction problem, or a meaning problem.

paragraphs.speaker: arthur
paragraphs.name: Arthur Brooks
paragraphs.text: And there's no evidence that young people enjoy their lives any less than you and me. On the contrary, they enjoy their lives more. There's no less satisfaction. I teach at Harvard, tons of satisfaction. That's the joy from accomplishments with struggle. You and I are satisfaction freaks. The trouble is when it comes to meaning, and especially people under 35, and doubly especially people in higher ed, who have all of the advantages. When you do the survey research and ask people, does your life feel meaningless? The explosion in the answer of yes came exactly in 2008, 2009. Fifteen percent of young people would say, I feel like my life is meaningless. 2008, boom, it goes up, exactly contemporaneously with the increases in depression, anxiety.

paragraphs.speaker: arthur
paragraphs.name: Arthur Brooks
paragraphs.text: By 2019, that was before the coronavirus epidemic, and then it got worse. Everything got worse. When everybody went home and we were even more isolated, even more on devices, even more living the way that life had started to change by 2008, that was amplified by the lockdowns. And then it actually weirdly didn't even get better when the coronavirus epidemic waned. It continued because the behavior changed. All of the societal patterns, the way that we're living our lives was exacerbated by the coronavirus epidemic, turned and metastasized into patterns of behavior that are very, very addictive. So now in this investigation, I'm saying this is a psychogenic epidemic, which means it's highly socially contagious, creates lots of misery, but has no obvious biological origin. But it turns out there are always neurochemical markers. There's always neuroscience behind the markers of what we see.

paragraphs.speaker: arthur
paragraphs.name: Arthur Brooks
paragraphs.text: The reason this disproportionately impacts young strivers is because all of the patterns, the neuroscientific patterns where we use our brains in a suboptimal way are most concentrated for people who don't remember the before times. Rich and Arthur remember the before times. And furthermore, for people who are entirely within the cone of a technologized striver hustle culture, what we see with people who are highly educated. Highly educated young people are most at risk for depression and anxiety because they're most likely to be using their brains in a way that's suboptimal for finding the meaning of life.

paragraphs.speaker: arthur
paragraphs.name: Arthur Brooks
paragraphs.text: There's a great neuroscientist, Ian McGilchrist, at Oxford. He brought back the idea of hemispheric lateralization, a fancy way of saying that the two sides of the brain do different things. The left side of the brain governs the complicated problems of life, the how to and what question, the technological stuff. When you want to write a piece of software or find your way from here to LA, you use the left side of your brain. The right side of the brain governs why questions, mystery, meaning, the things that we really care about. If the only questions you ever ask are being typed into a Google search bar, those are not meaning questions. Those are how and what questions. Chat GPT can't give you any information about questions that really matter for the meaning of your life. And that's the world that we're inhabiting.

paragraphs.speaker: arthur
paragraphs.name: Arthur Brooks
paragraphs.text: Here's where the penny dropped for me. When you're listening to young people talking about their misery, they'll keep talking about it again and again. Young people will say again and again, life doesn't feel real. It doesn't feel real. I feel like I'm in the matrix.
